request.question: Desert Ridge includes what non-profit medical practice facility based out of Minnesota?

title: Desert Ridge
Warrior Ice Arena
Mayo Clinic
Charles McClendon Practice Facility
LSU Indoor Practice Facility
Greenbranch Publishing
Michael Friisdahl
American College of Medical Practice Executives
William Worrall Mayo
Holy Name Medical Center
sentences: The Desert Ridge master-planned community comprises 5700 acre and is situated in the Northeast Valley of Phoenix, Arizona.
As one of Arizona’s largest master-planned communities, Desert Ridge could contain more than 50,000 residents.
The community includes CityNorth, Toscana of Desert Ridge, Desert Ridge Marketplace, JW Marriott Desert Ridge Resort & Spa, Mayo Clinic Hospital and American Express regional campus.
Warrior Arena is an ice hockey arena and practice facility in Brighton, Boston, Massachusetts.

The arena sits 600 and is part of a larger mixed-use development being constructed by New Balance at Boston Landing that includes the headquarters of New Balance and that will also include shops, a hotel and the practice facility of the Boston Bruins.
The arena has naming rights from the New Balance owned Warrior Sports which is the brand New Balance uses for their hockey products.

Warrior Arena is the home arena of the Boston Pride of the National Women's Hockey League and the practice facility of the Boston Bruins of the National Hockey League who moved in from the Bright-Landry Hockey Center and Ristuccia Ice Arena respectively.
The Mayo Clinic is a nonprofit medical practice and medical research group based in Rochester, Minnesota.
It employs more than 4,500 physicians and scientists and 57,100 allied health staff.
The practice specializes in treating difficult cases through tertiary care.
It spends over $660 million a year on research.
The Charles McClendon Practice Facility is the practice facility for LSU Tigers football.
The facility features the LSU Football Operations Center, the Tigers Indoor Practice Facility and four outdoor 100-yard football practice fields.
In 2002, it was named after former LSU head coach and College Football Hall of Fame member, Charles McClendon.

The LSU Indoor Practice Facility, built in 1991, is a climate-controlled 83,580 square feet facility connected to the Football Operations Center and adjacent to LSU's four outdoor 100-yard football practice fields.
It holds the 100-yd Anderson-Feazel LSU indoor field.
The playing surface is Momentum Field Turf by SportExe.
The indoor practice facility is adjacent to both the football-only weight room and LSU's four outdoor practice fields.
Besides allowing the team to practice during inclement weather, the indoor practice facility is used for LSU's summer endurance training and summer football camps.
Greenbranch Publishing is a privately held firm founded in 1998 and headquartered in Phoenix, Maryland.
It publishes medical practice management titles, electronic media, and audio conferences for physicians, practice administrators, and office practice managers.

The company is the publisher of "The Journal of Medical Practice Management", the "FAST Practice" newsletter, more than a dozen text books specializing on the business of medicine, and several medical practice web sites.
Michael Friisdahl is the President and Chief Executive Officer of Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment, a sports and entertainment company.

Friisdahl was appointed in October 2015, and is responsible for overseeing MLSE's sports properties which includes ownership of the Toronto Maple Leafs (National Hockey League), Toronto Raptors (National Basketball Association), Toronto FC (Major League Soccer), Toronto Marlies (American Hockey League), Raptors 905 (NBA Development League) and Toronto FC II (United Soccer League).

It also owns and operates Air Canada Centre, a multi-purpose indoor sporting arena in Toronto that has hosted more than 36 million fans over 2,500 events since its opening in 1999, and has invested in five other Toronto’s sports facilities — Ricoh Coliseum, home of the Marlies; BMO Field, home of Toronto FC; Lamport Stadium; the MasterCard Centre for Hockey Excellence, the practice facility for the Maple Leafs and Marlies and the BioSteel Centre, the practice facility of the Raptors.
MLSE operates and manages events at Ricoh Coliseum, BMO Field and BioSteel Centre.

The American College of Medical Practice Executives (ACMPE), established in 1956, supports and promotes the personal and professional growth of leaders to advance the medical practice management profession and is the certification and standard-setting body of the Medical Group Management Association (MGMA).
Both are headquartered in Englewood, Colo.
Nearly 6,000 members strong, ACMPE developed the standard-setting Body of Knowledge for Medical Practice Management.
It grants nationally recognized certification and fellowship designations to the most accomplished medical practice executives and leaders in the profession.
William Worrall Mayo ( ; May 31, 1819 – March 6, 1911) was a British-American medical doctor and chemist.
He is best known for establishing the private medical practice that later evolved into the Mayo Clinic.
He was a descendant of a famous English chemist, John Mayow.
His sons, William James Mayo and Charles Horace Mayo, established a joint medical practice in Rochester in the U.S. state of Minnesota in the 1880s.

Holy Name Medical Center is a fully accredited, not-for-profit healthcare facility based in Teaneck, Bergen County, New Jersey, United States, with off-site locations throughout Bergen, Hudson, and Passaic counties.
Founded and sponsored by the Sisters of St. Joseph of Peace in 1925, the comprehensive 361-bed medical center offers medical practice and technology.

With its growing "Korean Medical Program", Holy Name Medical Center has undertaken an effort to provide comprehensive health care services to underinsured and uninsured Korean patients from a wide area and also launched a similar "Chinese Medical Program" and "Filipino Medical Program" in 2015 as components of its larger "Asian Medical Program", with an "Asian Dementia Center" planned in 2016.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Desert Ridge includes what non-profit medical practice facility based out of Minnesota?
hop1_docs: Ascension (company) | Ascension is the world’s largest Catholic health system and the largest non-profit health system in the US with facilities in 23 states and the District of Columbia.  It is a faith-based collaboration of hospitals, medical practices, and innovators that shares best practices and the goal of creating healthier communities across the U.S. through community outreach and researching ways to cut healthcare costs.

American Board of Family Medicine | The American Board of Family Medicine (ABFM) is a non-profit, independent medical association of American physicians who practice in family medicine and its sub-specialties.  Founded in February 1969 as the American Board of Family Practice (ABFP), the group was the 20th medical specialty to be recognized by the American Board of Medical Specialties and was formed out of a need to encourage medical school graduates to enter general practice.  It adopted its current name in 2005.

InterRidge | InterRidge is a non-profit organisation that promotes interdisciplinary, international studies in the research of oceanic spreading centres, including mid-ocean ridge and back-arc basin systems.  It does so by creating a global research community, planning and coordinating new science programmes that no single nation can achieve alone, exchanging scientific information, and sharing new technologies and facilities.  InterRidge is dedicated to reaching out to the public, scientists and governments, and to providing a unified voice for ocean ridge researchers worldwide.

Mercy Medical Center – Dubuque | Mercy Medical Center – Dubuque is a non-profit medical facility located in Dubuque, Iowa.  It is one of two hospitals located in the city - the other being Finley Hospital.  The hospital is based primarily in Dubuque, with a critical access hospital in Dyersville, Iowa.

Carolinas HealthCare System Blue Ridge-Morganton | Carolinas HealthCare System Blue Ridge-Morganton is a not-for-profit hospital located in Morganton, North Carolina and serving Burke County, North Carolina.  The hospital anchors Carolinas HealthCare System Blue Ridge's system of healthcare providers that includes other facilities such as Valdese, Grace Ridge Retirement Community, Grace Heights Health and Rehabilitation Center, College Pines Health and Rehabilitation Center, Phifer Wellness Center and other physician practices.

Evidence-Based Veterinary Medical Association | The Evidence-Based Veterinary Medical Association (EBVMA) is an international, non-profit (501(c)3) professional organization founded with the mission of better organizing the emerging veterinary research, training, and practice of evidence-based veterinary medicine (EBVM) — the formal strategy to integrate the best critically designed and statistically evaluated research available combined with clinical expertise as well as the unique needs or wishes of each client in clinical practice.  EBVM draws from and parallels the evidence-based medicine movement in human medicine.

Hazelden Foundation | The Hazelden Foundation is a non-profit organization based in Center City, Minnesota.  Hazelden has alcohol and drug treatment facilities in Minnesota (Center City, Chaska, Maple Grove, Plymouth, and Saint Paul); Beaverton, Oregon; Newberg, Oregon; Chicago, Illinois; Naples, Florida and New York City, New York.  It offers assessment and primary residential addiction treatment for adults and youth, including extended care and intermediate care, as well as outpatient treatment, aftercare services and a family program.  In February 2014, it merged with the Betty Ford Center to form the Hazelden Betty Ford Foundation headquartered in Minnesota.
response.summary_1: Passages show: (a) "Hazelden Foundation is a non-profit organization based in Center City, Minnesota" and "Hazelden has alcohol and drug treatment facilities in Minnesota (Center City, Chaska, Maple Grove, Plymouth, and Saint Paul)" (passage [7]); (b) other non-profits listed (Ascension: "the world’s largest Catholic health system" (passage [1]); American Board of Family Medicine: "a non-profit, independent medical association" (passage [2]); Mercy Medical Center – Dubuque: "a non-profit medical facility located in Dubuque, Iowa" (passage [4]). Bridge/missing fact: confirm which of these (most likely the Hazelden Foundation) is the Minnesota-based non-profit that "Desert Ridge includes." Next retrieval should search the Desert Ridge page/entry for an explicit mention linking Desert Ridge to "Hazelden Foundation" or to a "non‑profit ... based in Center City, Minnesota" (use the quoted phrase above as an evidence anchor).
hop2_docs: Hazelden Betty Ford Foundation | The Hazelden Betty Ford Foundation is an addiction treatment and advocacy organization that was created in 2014 with the merger of the Minnesota-based Hazelden Foundation and the Betty Ford Center in Rancho Mirage, California in the United States.

Hazelden Foundation | The Hazelden Foundation is a non-profit organization based in Center City, Minnesota.  Hazelden has alcohol and drug treatment facilities in Minnesota (Center City, Chaska, Maple Grove, Plymouth, and Saint Paul); Beaverton, Oregon; Newberg, Oregon; Chicago, Illinois; Naples, Florida and New York City, New York.  It offers assessment and primary residential addiction treatment for adults and youth, including extended care and intermediate care, as well as outpatient treatment, aftercare services and a family program.  In February 2014, it merged with the Betty Ford Center to form the Hazelden Betty Ford Foundation headquartered in Minnesota.

Desert Ridge | The Desert Ridge master-planned community comprises 5700 acre and is situated in the Northeast Valley of Phoenix, Arizona.  As one of Arizona’s largest master-planned communities, Desert Ridge could contain more than 50,000 residents.  The community includes CityNorth, Toscana of Desert Ridge, Desert Ridge Marketplace, JW Marriott Desert Ridge Resort & Spa, Mayo Clinic Hospital and American Express regional campus.

Codependent No More | Codependent No More is the debut book of self-help author Melody Beattie.  It was originally published in 1986 by the publishing division of the Hazelden Foundation, and became a phenomenon of the self-help movement, going on to sell over eight million copies, six million copies of them in the United States.
Hedley Hazelden | Squadron Leader Hedley George "Hazel" Hazelden DFC and bar (7 June 1915 – August 2001) was a British test pilot.
Desert Ridge High School | Desert Ridge High School (DRHS) was founded in 2002 in Mesa, Arizona and is part of Gilbert Public Schools.

Desert Valley Charitable Foundation | The Desert Valley Charitable Foundation was founded in 1989.  The Foundation was established by Prem Reddy, MD, a cardiologist and philanthropist with an initial gift of $1 million.  Since that time, he has contributed over $5 million to the Foundation.  The Desert Valley Charitable Foundation is a 501(c)(3) not-for-profit charitable organization that serves the health care needs of the High Desert communities in many ways, including a free public health library, a scholarship program for students in the health care field, and the support of other health care related charities.
response.summary_2: Desert Ridge includes Mayo Clinic Hospital (passage [3]). Hazelden Foundation: no "included in Desert Ridge" provided in the supplied evidence.
